q: What causes a NullReferenceException and how do you avoid it?

answer.oneLiner: A NullReferenceException is thrown when you call a member on a reference that is null. Avoid it with null checks, `?.`, `??`, and nullable reference types that warn you at compile time.
answer.detail: Every reference type variable can hold null. When you call a method or access a property on null, the CLR cannot find the object and throws NullReferenceException. Common sources: method results like `FirstOrDefault()` that return null when nothing matches, uninitialized fields, and deserialization of JSON with missing properties. The null-conditional operator `?.` short-circuits to null without throwing. `??` supplies a fallback value if the left side is null. `??=` assigns the fallback only if the left side is null. Enabling nullable reference types moves null warnings to compile time. Never catch NullReferenceException - fix the root cause instead.
keyPoints: Cause: calling any member on a null reference - property access, method call, or indexer.
`?.` (null-conditional) short-circuits to null instead of throwing: `user?.Address?.City`.
`??` (null-coalescing) provides a fallback: `name ?? "Anonymous"`.
`??=` (null-coalescing assignment) assigns only if the left side is null: `cache ??= LoadData()`.
Enable nullable reference types to get compile-time warnings before null reaches runtime.

code.note: Chained `?.` stops at the first null and returns null; `??` supplies the fallback in one expression.

answer.analogy: A null reference is a door that is not there. `?.` checks if the door exists before trying to open it.
redFlags: Catching NullReferenceException to swallow it - this hides the bug instead of fixing it.
Sprinkling `!` (null-forgiving operator) everywhere to silence NRT warnings without fixing the null.
Not knowing that `?.` short-circuits the entire chain - `a?.B?.C` returns null if A or B is null.
Not enabling nullable reference types and relying on runtime crashes to find null issues.

followUpsQA.q: What is the null-conditional operator?
followUpsQA.a: `?.` - it accesses the member only if the left side is not null; otherwise returns null for the whole expression.

followUpsQA.q: What does ?? do?
followUpsQA.a: Returns the left side if it is not null; otherwise returns the right side. `name ?? "default"` returns name unless it is null.

followUpsQA.q: Should you catch NullReferenceException?
followUpsQA.a: No - catching it hides the bug. Find and fix the null at its source.

followUpsQA.q: How do nullable reference types help?
followUpsQA.a: The compiler uses flow analysis to warn you at build time when you might dereference a nullable reference without checking it first.

followUpsQA.q: What is the null-forgiving operator?
followUpsQA.a: `name!` suppresses the NRT warning and tells the compiler you know the value is not null. Use it sparingly only when you have external knowledge the compiler lacks.

dialogue.text: What causes a NullReferenceException and how do you avoid it?

You get a NRE when you call any member - method, property, or indexer - on a reference that is null. Common sources are `FirstOrDefault` returning null when nothing matches, uninitialized fields, and missing JSON properties during deserialization.
What is the most useful operator for avoiding it?

The null-conditional `?.`. Instead of `user.Address.City` throwing if user is null, `user?.Address?.City` returns null without throwing. Chain `??` to provide a fallback: `user?.Address?.City ?? "Unknown"`.
What is the difference between ?? and ??=?

`??` returns a fallback value inline. `??=` assigns the fallback to the variable itself only if it is null: `cache ??= LoadFromDatabase()`. This is the lazy initialisation pattern without a full if block.
What about nullable reference types?

Enabling NRT with `<Nullable>enable</Nullable>` moves the problem from runtime to compile time. The compiler uses flow analysis to warn when you access a member on a potentially null reference. On a brownfield project I enabled it file by file and caught three real null-dereference bugs in the first day.
What is the wrong way to handle NRE?

Catching it and swallowing it. That hides the bug and the code continues in an unknown state. Also using the null-forgiving `!` to silence compiler warnings - that is just deferring the crash to runtime. Fix the null at its source.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Are null and an empty string the same thing in C#?

answer.oneLiner: No. `null` means there is no string object at all. `""` (the same as `string.Empty`) is a valid zero-length string object. They are different values and behave differently.
answer.detail: A null string has no object reference - calling `.Length` on it throws NullReferenceException. An empty string `""` is a valid string object with `Length == 0`. `""` and `string.Empty` are the same interned instance. `string.IsNullOrEmpty(s)` returns true for both null and empty. `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(s)` additionally returns true for strings that contain only whitespace, which is usually what you want for user input validation. When comparing strings to empty, prefer `string.IsNullOrEmpty` over `s ==
` because it handles the null case without a NullReferenceException.
keyPoints: `null` means no string object exists - `s.Length` throws NRE; `"".Length` is 0.
`""` and `string.Empty` are the same interned empty string instance - they are equal by value and reference.
`string.IsNullOrEmpty(s)` is true for both null and `""`.
`string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(s)` is also true for `" "`, `"\t"`, and `"\n"` - use this for user input.
Comparing `s == ""` is safe (string overloads `==`) but `s.Length == 0` crashes if s is null.

code.note: Always use IsNullOrWhiteSpace for user-facing input validation - it handles the invisible whitespace case too.

answer.analogy: null is an empty envelope slot. An empty string is a slot with an empty envelope inside.
redFlags: Treating null and empty string as interchangeable - they are distinct values with different behaviour.
Calling `.Length` or any method on a string without checking for null first.
Using `s.Equals("")` instead of `string.IsNullOrEmpty(s)` - the Equals call throws if s is null.
Not knowing that `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace` also handles strings with only spaces and tabs.

followUpsQA.q: What does calling .Length on a null string do?
followUpsQA.a: Throws NullReferenceException - there is no object to call a method on.

followUpsQA.q: Is string.Empty the same object as ""?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - both are the same interned empty string instance; `object.ReferenceEquals("", string.Empty)` is true.

followUpsQA.q: What does IsNullOrWhiteSpace check that IsNullOrEmpty does not?
followUpsQA.a: It also returns true for strings that contain only whitespace characters like spaces, tabs, and newlines.

followUpsQA.q: Is comparing a string to null with == safe?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - string overloads `==` to use value comparison. `s == null` is safe even if s is null.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between null and default for a string?
followUpsQA.a: They are the same - `default(string)` is null because string is a reference type.

dialogue.text: Are null and an empty string the same in C#?

No - they are completely different. Null means no string object exists at all. An empty string is a valid object with Length zero. Calling `.Length` on null throws NullReferenceException; calling it on `""` returns 0.
How do you check for both safely?

`string.IsNullOrEmpty(s)` returns true for both null and empty string. For user input I use `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(s)` which also catches strings with only spaces and tabs - common in form submissions.
Is string.Empty the same object as the empty string literal?
Yes - both are the same interned instance. The CLR interns common strings. `ReferenceEquals("", string.Empty)` returns true. There is no performance benefit to preferring one over the other.
Is it safe to write s == null?

Yes - the string type overloads `==` to use value comparison so `null == null` returns true and no NRE is thrown. `s.Equals(null)` is different - if s is null, `Equals` throws NRE, so always prefer `s == null` or `s is null`.
Give me a practical scenario where the difference matters.

In a SaaS app, a user submits a registration form with no email address. The controller may receive null (field not submitted) or empty string (field submitted blank). Both mean the email is missing, so `IsNullOrWhiteSpace` handles both cases with one check. Treating them differently causes bugs where one path validates and the other does not.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Can an enum use inheritance in C#?

answer.oneLiner: No. Every enum implicitly derives from `System.Enum` and is sealed. You cannot inherit from an enum or extend it with more values through inheritance.
answer.detail: Enums are value types that implicitly derive from `System.Enum`. They are sealed so subclassing is impossible. The only 'inheritance' allowed is specifying the underlying numeric type: `enum Priority : byte` tells the runtime to store the value as a byte. The `[Flags]` attribute allows bitwise combination of enum values but is not inheritance. To add behaviour to an enum, use extension methods. For richer enum-like types that carry behaviour, use the smart-enum pattern - a class with static readonly instances that each have their own behaviour methods.
keyPoints: Enums are sealed value types that derive from `System.Enum` - no subclassing from another enum.
The only type specification allowed is the underlying integral type: `enum Status : byte`.
`[Flags]` enables bitwise combination of values but is not inheritance.
To add behaviour, use extension methods on the enum type.
For richer enum-like types with methods and polymorphism, use the smart-enum class pattern.

code.note: Extension methods are the idiomatic way to add behaviour to enums without a smart-enum pattern.

answer.analogy: An enum is a set of named exits on a motorway - you can label them but you cannot add a new exit by renaming the motorway.
redFlags: Thinking you can inherit from an enum to add more values - enums are sealed.
Confusing `enum Status : byte` (underlying type) with inheritance from another enum.
Not knowing the `[Flags]` attribute exists for bitwise enum combinations.
Not knowing extension methods are the idiomatic way to add behaviour to enums.

followUpsQA.q: What does `enum E : byte` mean?
followUpsQA.a: It sets the underlying storage type to byte (0-255) instead of the default int. Useful for compact enums in serialisation or protocols.

followUpsQA.q: What does [Flags] add?
followUpsQA.a: `[Flags]` marks an enum as a bit field where values can be combined with bitwise OR. Values must be powers of 2 for this to work correctly.

followUpsQA.q: How do you add behaviour to an enum?
followUpsQA.a: Extension methods - define a static method in a static class with `this EnumType` as the first parameter.

followUpsQA.q: What is the smart-enum pattern?
followUpsQA.a: A class with private constructor and static readonly instances, each carrying custom behaviour. Used when enum values need methods that vary per value.

followUpsQA.q: Can you combine two enums of different types?
followUpsQA.a: No - you cannot combine or inherit between enum types. You can convert between the underlying numeric types explicitly.

dialogue.text: Can an enum use inheritance in C#?

No. Enums are sealed value types that implicitly derive from `System.Enum`. You cannot inherit from another enum or add values by subclassing. The only customisation is specifying the underlying integral type like `enum Priority : byte`.
What can you do with the underlying type?

You can set it to any integral type: byte, short, int (default), long, and their unsigned equivalents. For a status code sent in a network protocol we used `enum PacketType : ushort` to map directly to the wire format and save space.
What is the [Flags] attribute?

`[Flags]` tells the runtime the enum is a bit field - values can be combined with bitwise OR. `Permission.Read | Permission.Write` gives a combined value. Values must be powers of 2 for it to work correctly, and you add a `None = 0` member as the empty state.
How do you add behaviour to an enum?

Extension methods. `public static bool IsTerminal(this OrderStatus s) => s is Shipped or Cancelled;` reads naturally at the call site: `order.Status.IsTerminal()`. For richer polymorphism where each value needs different logic, I use the smart-enum pattern - a class with static readonly instances.
Give me a real case for smart enums.

In a logistics app, each shipment carrier had a different tracking URL format. An enum cannot hold that logic. A `Carrier` smart-enum class with a virtual `GetTrackingUrl(string trackingNumber)` method let each carrier override it. `carrier.GetTrackingUrl("1Z999")` returned the correct URL per carrier without a switch statement.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Explain the ternary operator and the null-coalescing operators (?? and ??=).

answer.oneLiner: `cond ? a : b` picks one of two values based on a boolean condition. `a ?? b` returns `a` unless it is null, then returns `b`. `a ??= b` assigns `b` to `a` only if `a` is currently null.
answer.detail: `?:` is the ternary conditional operator - it evaluates the condition and returns one of two expressions. It is not null-specific. `??` is the null-coalescing operator - it only reacts to null, not to false or zero. It is right-associative so you can chain: `x ?? y ?? z` returns the first non-null. `??=` is the compound assignment form: it assigns the right side to the left variable only if the left is currently null. Together with `?.`, these form a concise null-handling idiom: `user?.Name ?? "Guest"`.
keyPoints: `condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse` - evaluates condition, returns one of two expressions.
`a ?? b` returns `a` if it is not null; otherwise returns `b`. Does not react to false or empty.
`a ??= b` assigns `b` to `a` only when `a` is null - the lazy initialisation pattern in one operator.
`??` is right-associative: `x ?? y ?? z` is `x ?? (y ?? z)` and returns the first non-null.
Pairs naturally with `?.`: `order?.Customer?.Name ?? "Anonymous"`.

code.note: `??` and `?.` compose cleanly; `??=` is the concise lazy initialisation pattern without a full if block.

answer.analogy: ?? is a safety net - it only catches you when you fall (are null), not when you stumble (are false).
redFlags: Confusing `??` (reacts to null only) with `||` (reacts to any falsy value like false, 0, empty string).
Not knowing `??` is right-associative and can be chained: `x ?? y ?? z`.
Using a full if statement for lazy initialisation when `??=` makes it one line.
Not knowing `?.` and `??` compose: `a?.B ?? fallback` is a single safe null-chain expression.

followUpsQA.q: Does ?? react to false or 0?
followUpsQA.a: No - `??` only reacts to null. A bool value of false passes through as false, not as the fallback.

followUpsQA.q: What does ??= do?
followUpsQA.a: Assigns the right side to the left variable only if the left side is currently null - one-line lazy initialisation.

followUpsQA.q: Is ?? chainable?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - it is right-associative. `a ?? b ?? c` returns a if not null, else b if not null, else c.

followUpsQA.q: Can you use ?? with value types?
followUpsQA.a: Only with nullable value types like `int?`. `int? x = null; int y = x ?? 0;` returns 0.

followUpsQA.q: How does ternary differ from an if statement?
followUpsQA.a: Ternary is an expression that returns a value and can be used inline. An if statement is a statement and cannot be used where a value is expected.

dialogue.text: Explain the ternary operator and the null-coalescing operators.

`cond ? a : b` picks one of two values based on a boolean - it is not null-specific. `a ?? b` returns `a` unless it is null, then returns `b`. `a ??= b` assigns `b` to `a` only if `a` is currently null - that is the lazy initialisation pattern in one operator.
How is ?? different from using || in JavaScript or Python?

In JavaScript `||` is falsy-based: `0 || 'default'` gives `'default'` because 0 is falsy. C# `??` is strictly null-based: `0 ?? fallback` gives `0` because 0 is not null. This is important - a zero price or false flag should not fall through to a default.
Can you chain ???
Yes - `??` is right-associative. `primaryCity ?? secondaryCity ?? "Unknown"` returns the first non-null. It is clean for fallback chains where multiple sources might have the value.
Give me a real use of ??=.

Lazy cache initialisation: `private List<Product>? _cache; _cache ??= _db.Products.ToList();`. On the first call it loads from the database and assigns to `_cache`. On subsequent calls `_cache` is not null so it is returned immediately. One line instead of a full if-null-assign block.
How do ?. and ?? compose?

`order?.Customer?.Name ?? "Guest"` - the `?.` chain evaluates to null if any step is null; `??` then provides the fallback. It is null-safe from object to property to nested property in one expression, which is far cleaner than nested if checks.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: What is the difference between early binding and late binding?

answer.oneLiner: Early (static) binding resolves member calls at compile time with full type checking. Late binding resolves them at runtime via `dynamic` or reflection - no compile-time verification.
answer.detail: With early binding, the compiler knows the exact type and verifies that the called member exists, which gives IntelliSense, compile-time errors for typos, and faster execution. With late binding, the type or member is resolved at runtime. In C# this happens via the `dynamic` keyword (uses the DLR) or via reflection (`Type.GetMethod`, `MethodInfo.Invoke`). Late binding is slower because the lookup happens at runtime and errors only surface as exceptions. Virtual dispatch (calling an overridden method through a base type reference) is still early binding - the compiler knows the signature, only the concrete implementation is chosen at runtime by the vtable.
keyPoints: Early binding: compiler resolves member at compile time; IntelliSense and type-checking work.
Late binding: member resolved at runtime via `dynamic` or reflection; errors surface at runtime.
Virtual dispatch is NOT late binding - the signature is compile-time known; only the implementation is runtime-selected.
Late binding is slower: DLR or reflection resolution adds overhead that compile-time dispatch avoids.
Use late binding only when the type or member is genuinely unknown at compile time: plugins, COM, scripting.

code.note: Late binding via reflection is the right tool for unknown-at-compile-time types in plugin systems; for known types always use early binding.

answer.analogy: Early binding is a GPS with a fixed map. Late binding is asking for directions at each junction - slower and you might get lost.
redFlags: Saying virtual dispatch is late binding - the compiler knows the signature; only the vtable lookup is runtime.
Using `dynamic` for a type you know at compile time - you lose all safety for no benefit.
Not knowing that reflection errors only surface at runtime as `TargetInvocationException` or `NullReferenceException`.
Not knowing that `dynamic` uses the DLR which is different from raw reflection.

followUpsQA.q: Is virtual method dispatch late binding?
followUpsQA.a: No - the method signature is known at compile time. Only the concrete override is chosen at runtime via the vtable. The compiler verifies the call.

followUpsQA.q: What runtime exceptions does late binding produce?
followUpsQA.a: `RuntimeBinderException` for dynamic; `TargetInvocationException` wrapping the actual error for reflection.

followUpsQA.q: When is late binding necessary?
followUpsQA.a: When the type or member is not known until runtime - plugin architectures, COM interop, scripting engines, and dynamic data sources.

followUpsQA.q: Is dynamic faster than reflection?
followUpsQA.a: Dynamic uses the DLR which caches call-site binders after the first resolution, making repeated calls faster than raw reflection. But both are slower than static dispatch.

followUpsQA.q: How do you make late binding safer?
followUpsQA.a: Define an interface or base class that the late-bound type must implement, then cast to that interface after loading. This gives you early-bound access to the agreed API surface.

dialogue.text: What is the difference between early binding and late binding?

Early binding resolves member calls at compile time. The compiler checks that the method or property exists, IntelliSense works, and a typo is a build error. Late binding resolves at runtime via `dynamic` or reflection - no compile-time verification, errors are runtime exceptions.
Is virtual dispatch late binding?

No. The compiler knows the method signature at compile time and verifies the call. At runtime the vtable selects the concrete override. The signature is fixed early; only the implementation varies. Late binding means the compiler does not know the method exists at all.
When would late binding be the right choice?

Plugin architectures - loading a payment gateway DLL at runtime where the type is unknown at compile time. Or COM interop. Or a scripting engine where method names come from user input. In all these cases the type cannot be referenced in code, so reflection or dynamic is the only option.
How do you make a late-bound plugin safe?

Define an interface the plugin must implement - `IPaymentGateway`. Load the assembly at runtime, find the type, create an instance, and cast to `IPaymentGateway`. From that point all calls are early-bound through the interface. You use late binding only to discover and instantiate the type, not to call its methods.
What is the performance difference?

Dynamic uses the DLR which caches binders after the first call so repeated calls on the same type are fast but still slower than a static dispatch. Raw reflection has no such cache. A static virtual call is a vtable lookup - nanoseconds. A DLR call is microseconds. In a hot path processing millions of records, that matters.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
